grammar.title: Joint subject ‘Pablo y Timoteo’
grammar.detail: Letters frequently open with a co-sender. Spanish, like English, lists names with ‘y’ — verbs that would follow take a plural form (here none — the salutation is verbless).

grammar.title: ‘junto con’
grammar.detail: ‘together with’ — a fixed prepositional phrase, often used to extend a list to a final, slightly distinct group.

expression.note: Both writers describe themselves as ‘siervos’ — slaves, not employees. Notably, Paul does not invoke ‘apostle’ here, perhaps because Philippi was a uniquely warm relationship.

grammar.title: ‘Que’ + subjunctive (wish / blessing)
grammar.detail: Identical to Eph 1:2. ‘Que Dios… les concedan…’ The opening ‘Que’ + subjunctive expresses ‘May God grant…’

expression.note: Paul’s recurring greeting. Notice he uses it word-for-word across letters — a deliberate trademark.

grammar.title: acordarse + de
grammar.detail: ‘to remember’ in Spanish almost always uses the reflexive form acordarse + de + object. Compare: ‘recordar algo’ (transitive, more formal).

grammar.title: cada vez que + indicative
grammar.detail: ‘every time that’ with a habitual present takes the indicative: ‘cada vez que me acuerdo’. (If it pointed to a future, possibly subjunctive.)

expression.phrase: Doy gracias a mi Dios cada vez que me acuerdo de ustedes
expression.note: Philippi was Paul’s favourite church. The verse sets a tone of warmth that runs throughout the letter.
